headline_of_the_day: Hacker wipes Romania's land registry database in cyberattack

key_points.text: A hacker wiped Romania's land registry database, disrupting property records for the entire country.
Russian intelligence hijacked security cameras across Europe and Ukraine to monitor NATO military logistics and weapons shipments.
Hackers stole a significant amount of data from Craneware, a tech firm used by thousands of US hospitals and pharmacies for billing.
The European Union fined AliExpress $629 million for failing to stop sales of illegal and counterfeit products, the largest penalty under new digital services rules.
US gas prices returned to an average of $4 per gallon as Middle East tensions drove oil prices higher.

stories.title: Amazon breaks Apple Wallet feature that millions rely on
stories.summary: The company disabled a recently added Wallet integration, disrupting a feature users depend on for storing tickets and passes.

stories.title: AMD ships first rack AI system to compete with Nvidia, adds Microsoft as customer
stories.summary: The Helios system marks AMD's entry into complete rack-scale AI infrastructure with immediate enterprise adoption.

article.background: Nvidia makes most of the specialized chips that companies use to train and run artificial intelligence systems. AMD, a competitor, has been working to offer an alternative. This new move expands AMD's lineup and brings a major customer on board.
article.what_happened: AMD announced Helios, its first complete AI system designed for data centers. The system combines AMD's Instinct processors, which handle AI tasks, with AMD EPYC processors, which handle other server functions. Microsoft said it will use these systems in Azure, its cloud service, making it a major customer for AMD's technology.
article.why_it_matters: When multiple companies make AI chips, customers get more choices and typically better pricing. For people using cloud AI services, more competition means lower costs and more options to choose from. AMD's move signals it is ready to challenge Nvidia's dominance in AI infrastructure.

stories.title: Electric air taxis move from concept to military deployment
stories.summary: Archer and Anduril unveiled an autonomous vertical takeoff aircraft designed for both commercial and defense use, backed by military contracts.

article.background: Archer Aviation makes electric aircraft designed to carry passengers in cities. The company is now partnering with Anduril, a defense technology company, to build military versions of these aircraft.
article.what_happened: The two companies unveiled Thunder, an autonomous military aircraft they developed together. Thunder is designed to work alongside helicopters as a support drone. Archer's stock price rose 18% after the announcement.
article.why_it_matters: Electric aircraft technology is expanding beyond passenger transport into defense uses. Military contracts can speed up progress on this technology, which may eventually reach commercial markets.

stories.title: Trump administration moves to restrict access to frontier AI models
stories.summary: The White House is preparing controls on who can access the most advanced AI systems domestically, citing concerns about Chinese competition.

stories.title: AI system disproves 87-year-old math conjecture in hours
stories.summary: A mathematician used Claude to resolve the Jacobian Conjecture, a problem that had remained open since 1939, surprising the mathematics community.

stories.title: US gas prices return to $4 per gallon as Middle East conflict escalates
stories.summary: The national average reached $4 a gallon again after US and Iran launched attacks, pushing oil prices sharply higher.

stories.title: Oil prices touch $90 despite months of US-Iran conflict
stories.summary: Energy markets have remained relatively stable even as Middle East fighting intensified, with analysts citing ample global supply.

stories.title: EU fines AliExpress $629 million for selling illegal products
stories.summary: The European Commission issued a record penalty after the platform failed to comply with orders to remove counterfeit and unsafe items under new digital services law.

article.background: AliExpress is an online marketplace owned by Alibaba. Third-party sellers use it to sell products around the world. The European Union has a rule called the Digital Services Act that requires online platforms to stop the sale of illegal and counterfeit goods.
article.what_happened: The EU fined AliExpress 550 million euros, or about $629 million. This is the largest penalty under the Digital Services Act so far. The fine came after AliExpress did not remove counterfeit and unsafe items when the EU ordered it to do so.
article.why_it_matters: The fine signals that the EU will enforce its rules against illegal goods sold online. If you buy from AliExpress or similar platforms, the EU is pushing to make those marketplaces safer by removing fake products and items that fail safety standards. The penalty suggests platforms will face serious costs if they ignore these requirements.

stories.title: Czechia bans mobile phones in schools starting September 2027
stories.summary: The country set a firm date for a nationwide classroom phone prohibition affecting millions of students.

stories.title: FDA retracts test result but maintains link between Taylor Farms lettuce and parasite outbreak
stories.summary: The agency walked back a positive test for cyclospora but still connects the company's lettuce to illnesses across multiple states and restaurant chains.

article.background: Cyclosporiasis is a foodborne illness caused by a parasite that can contaminate fresh produce like lettuce. The FDA has been investigating an outbreak linked to lettuce from Taylor Farms, a produce supplier. As part of that investigation, the agency tested samples from the company's products.
article.what_happened: The FDA found cyclospora in one Taylor Farms lettuce sample and initially reported a positive test result. The agency then retracted that test, saying it was a false positive. However, the FDA still connects Taylor Farms lettuce to cyclosporiasis cases across multiple states and restaurant chains, including Taco Bell. Iceberg lettuce has been recalled in 27 states due to potential cyclospora contamination.
article.why_it_matters: If you buy pre-packaged salads or lettuce, especially iceberg lettuce from the recalled areas, this outbreak may have affected the products available to you. The FDA's investigation is still ongoing, so paying attention to recall notices and avoiding products from recalled batches remains important for your health.

stories.title: Five cups of coffee per day confirmed safe for most adults
stories.summary: A large study validated that up to 400 milligrams of caffeine daily does not pose health risks for the majority of people.

stories.title: Hacker wipes Romania's entire land registry database
stories.summary: The attack destroyed property records for the country, with immediate consequences for real estate transactions and ownership verification.

stories.title: Russian intelligence hacks security cameras to spy on NATO military operations
stories.summary: Netherlands intelligence services confirmed Russia is systematically hijacking internet-connected cameras across Europe to watch weapons shipments to Ukraine and troop movements.

stories.title: Hackers breach tech firm used by thousands of US hospitals and pharmacies
stories.summary: Edinburgh-based Craneware confirmed a significant data theft during a cyberattack, potentially exposing health information from its billing software used across the US healthcare system.

stories.title: Hackers exploit recently patched WordPress bugs affecting millions of websites
stories.summary: Two critical flaws in WordPress gave attackers remote takeover access to tens of millions of sites whose owners have not yet updated.
